insight: Buying Redeemer simultaneously with Antima Comet is a 'cute maneuver' but probably not worth it unless you are launching a maximum comet anyway. The comparison works out to roughly (mature Redeemer + 1 damage) vs. (11 engineers + a green), putting the Redeemer at a moderate deficit on paper, though surplus engineers may overdefend for several turns making it closer.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of whether buying Redeemer and Antima Comet simultaneously is optimal versus delaying Redeemer for a bigger Antima.

insight: When buying Redeemer simultaneously with Antima Comet, the Antima's engineers arrive just in time to soak the Redeemer charges, making the combo relatively flat to inflation. However, at high inflation the surplus engineers are less likely to overdefend, which would favor delaying the Redeemer and going for a bigger Antima instead.
author: .bky_1556
context: Detailed turn-by-turn analysis of the Redeemer+Antima Comet simultaneous purchase scenario.

insight: In sets with Gauss Fabricator, Player 1 can generally get Fabricator on turn 2 without floating gold, whereas Player 2 floats 2 gold to match. As a response to P1's clean fast Fabricator, P2 can try DD, ECC, DDD, Fab — ending up half a turn behind on Fabricator but with two more drones as compensation, which is roughly the standard trade-off for initiative.
author: spiritfryer
context: Post-game discussion of Gauss Fabricator race openings and Player 1 vs Player 2 lines.

insight: If you need green first as Player 1, the best options are: (1) DD then DC, which is especially good with Fission Turret; or (2) DD, DD, DCC if multiple green sources are desirable (e.g., Tantalum Ray, Plasmafier). A third-engineer line of DD, DDE, DDD, DDDC is also fine if a third engineer is accessible.
author: mqp
context: Discussion of Player 1 opening lines when green resources are needed early.

insight: The pre-nerf Redeemer was considered problematic primarily because of a very natural Player 2 line to rush it out, giving P2 a disproportionate advantage (estimated ~58% or potentially 65%+ P2 win rate in true Redeemer mirrors). The issue was the asymmetry it created for P2 rather than the unit being generically too strong.
author: liadahlia
context: Community discussion about Redeemer's nerf and P1/P2 win rate disparities in Redeemer mirrors.

insight: Player-favored sets are rare, and when they occur the disadvantaged player can almost always still win. Overall P2 win rate is only about 52%, which is a relatively small advantage, and extreme set-based disparities tend to cancel out across many games.
author: consensus
context: Extended community debate about P1/P2 win rate advantages and whether certain sets are effectively auto-wins.

insight: Gambiting should be done more aggressively than most players default to. The correct mental model is to treat gambling your defender as the default and look for reasons not to, rather than treating defending as the default. However, this is advanced enough that players below ~1700 rating should focus their mental energy elsewhere, as correctly identifying and calculating gambits in fast time controls is difficult.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of 'always gambit' as a strategic heuristic and its applicability at different skill levels.

insight: Clicked Militia provides approximately the value of half a damage. It is acceptable as a first attacker and good when it exploits opponent's defense gaps, but is not efficient enough to plan around using repeatedly.
author: p0lari
context: Game review discussion about double Militia purchase and its value relative to other attackers.

insight: A common mistake is building an Animus for Tarsiers when you already have three Blastforges and are floating blue. Tarsier is only marginally more efficient than Steelsplitter, not enough to justify the 6-gold Animus cost or the floating blue — spending that 6 gold on an extra Steelsplitter is better in most cases.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of offense selection when a player has high blue production and is floating resources.

insight: Tarsiers are better than Steelsplitters specifically when you are trying to saturate an Apollo, because Tarsiers benefit more from the Apollo's ability to produce red efficiently.
author: .bky_1556
context: Follow-up to discussion on when Animus+Tarsier is better than Steelsplitters when floating blue.

insight: Against a true aggressive red opening, the correct response is to buy 0–1 Thorium Dynamo rather than 3. The wiki suggestion of buying 3 Dynamos against aggressive openings only applies when going breachproof; in that BP context, 3 Dynamos is correct though sometimes only 2 are affordable.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion reviewing the Thorium Dynamo wiki page and correcting its guidance on aggressive red openings.

insight: An Evil Animus is a strong punish against double Thorium Dynamo openings, even in sets with Electrovore, because the opponent's clunky early economy is exposed to the aggressive tempo of Evil Animus.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion of Thorium Dynamo counterplay and corrections to wiki guidance.

insight: In a set with Drake, buying a third Drake when you only plan to have two Blastforges is generally wrong — two Steelsplitters are better value. The main exception is if you need exactly 3 breach damage for a tactical breakthrough, or if Steelsplitter supply is a concern. Having three Blastforges makes a third Drake worthwhile for tempo, as the two extra threat forces significant defensive spending.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of when buying a third Drake is correct relative to Steelsplitters.

insight: In a Plasmafier set as Player 1, a strong opening line is DD / DD / DDB / 1DC, enabling a quick Plasmafier purchase. After getting it out, you defend for one turn while adding a Tesla or a second Plasmafier. If green production seems excessive, a slower turn-4 of DD instead of 1DC is an alternative, especially if a Wall is needed on turn 5.
author: apooche
context: Replay review of a Plasmafier and Tesla set where the poster asked how to improve their play.

insight: In Prismata notation, base-set units are commonly abbreviated by their hotkey. 'FF' is therefore ambiguous — it could mean two Forcefields or one Steelforge (abbreviated F) clicked twice. Using 'FF' to mean Steelforge is considered misleading by experienced players.
author: p0lari
context: Clarification thread about abbreviation conventions after a notation ambiguity caused confusion.
